### Quick Take

An Instagram reel is promoting *Apamarga* root extract for the management of kidney stones. After fact-checking, we discovered that the claim is **mostly false**.

Rating Mostly False <image:9799> 
### The Claim

A popular [Instagram post](https://www.instagram.com/reel/DL2ygCoSc3w/?igsh=MWNyZWxjMGUzbnhwag%3D%3D) claims that consuming *Apamarga* root extract on an empty stomach may dissolve kidney stones of any size. Even if many employ natural remedies to get rid of renal calculi, setting a deadline for their eradication might be very misleading.

Apamarga and kidney stones <image:9800> 
### Fact Check

## What causes kidney stones to develop? At what point do they turn into a concern?

[Kidney stones are mineral precipitates](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5685519/) in the renal calyces and pelvis that are either free or attached to the renal papillae. They originate when urine becomes supersaturated with [both organic and crystalline components](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/322935596_Kidney_Stone_Disease_An_Update_on_Current_Concepts), such as calcium, oxalate, or uric acid. Most stones are usually composed of calcium oxalate. These are usually caused by the following conditions:

* Dehydration
* Excessive consumption of protein and sodium
* struggling to regulate weight
* Specific metabolic and genetic issues

Even though smaller kidney calculi are typically painless (asymptomatic), larger ones can impede urinary flow, causing severe discomfort (renal colic), nausea, vomiting, and even blood in the urine. Pain from [a kidney stone](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK442014/) typically starts in the side or lower back and might spread to the groin as the stone passes through the urinary system.

If the issue is not addressed appropriately, it may result in serious complications; therefore, if there is any discomfort, one should consult a doctor right away.

## Is *Apamarga* root extract a reliable remedy for kidney stones?

No, not always. *Apamarga*, also known as the prickly chaff-flower, is a widely used Ayurvedic herb. The traditional medicine utilises the plant’s root and ash (*kshara*) for its diuretic, anti-inflammatory, and alkaline characteristics. Its [high bioactive profile](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22557217/) (flavanoids and saponins) may also have a major impact on the urinary biochemistry.

Although the saponin-rich fraction of *Apamarga* may facilitate and [encourage the breakdown of CaOx crystals](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22439435/), these results are limited to preliminary experimental investigations. Furthermore, because there is a dearth of high-quality clinical studies, the formulation should not be used in clinical practice for all stone types or sizes. Most significantly, *Apamarga*‘s root extract may not work for larger aggregates, staghorn calculi, cystine stones, or radiopaque infection stones; instead, urological intervention or other medical dissolving techniques may be essential.

![Dr Sharad Malhotra, Gastroenterologist](https://static.thip.media/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/12160555/Sharad-Malhotra-edited.jpg)

While home remedies could be beneficial for digestive or urinary health, **Dr. Sharad Malhotra, HOD Gastroenterology, Hepatology & Therapeutic Endoscopy, Aakash Healthcare, New Delhi**, advises against utilising them as a long-term kidney stone [treatment](https://www.thip.media/treatment/ "List of Treatment Guides"). Additionally, **Dr. Malhotra** states that these unregulated remedies shouldn’t be considered a substitute for conventional medical therapies for renal calculi because they lack strong scientific support. 
 
*Apamarga* root extract may therefore be used extensively due to its possible anti-nephrolithiasis (stone-dissolving) and diuretic qualities. However, until thorough clinical trials determine its safety, dosage, and efficacy, it should only be used as an adjuvant or complement to evidence-based medical therapy for renal calculi.

## Can smaller kidney stones dissolve in 2–3 days, or larger ones in 10–15 days?

No, absolutely not. This is because claims [that kidney stones resolve](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2907772/) on their own in a few days are unsupported by scientific data. For asymptomatic calculi less than 5 mm, it usually takes weeks instead of days. Additionally, the symptomatic stones, which range in size from 5 to 10 mm, require cautious treatment for a few weeks to a month.

It is crucial to keep in mind that the primary basis for addressing kidney stones is an appropriate clinical evaluation; self-diagnosis and medication are not advised.

## Why are online kidney stone remedies getting popular? Are they an acceptable means to resolve this issue?

High-risk patients are compelled to seek [unproven at-home options](https://www.thip.media/health-news-fact-check/fact-check-can-garlic-and-ginger-dissolve-kidney-stones-in-21-days/130880/) because they:

* Fear recurrence
* Are accessible and reasonably priced alternatives
* Help bypass the doctor’s visit

Even while some of these therapies may provide short-term symptom relief, they should not be considered a primary intervention for kidney stones. Further concerns regarding the effectiveness of these unverified natural remedies are raised by the lack of comprehensive clinical research to establish the optimal dosage.

![Dr. Archana Daftardar](https://static.thip.media/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/03194136/IMG-20250731-WA0014-1.jpg)

**Dr. Archana Daftardar, a Consultant Nephrologist, Hypertension Specialist, and Transplant Physician at Renova Hospitals in Kompally, Hyderabad**, further supports this by pointing out that there are many misconceptions about kidney stone prevention and treatment. Unsubstantiated alternative therapies might actually make the illness worse rather than better. It is advised to seek advice from licensed Ayurvedic or allopathic specialists.

Thus, depending [only on unapproved remedies](https://www.thip.media/health-news-fact-check/fact-check-are-lemon-water-and-rock-salt-effective-for-kidney-stones/131468/) may cause complications and delay the required therapy. [Home-based therapies](https://www.thip.media/health-news-fact-check/fact-check-can-ajwain-water-with-sugar-help-dissolve-kidney-stones/129736/) should only be employed as supportive care under professional supervision; timely medical evaluation is necessary to determine the best course of action.

## In what ways do clinicians handle renal calculi?

The [management of kidney stones](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK348939/) depends on:

* Calculi dimensions
* Chemical composition
* Anatomical placement

For small verified cases, specialists may recommend dietary modifications, analgesics, and hydration therapy. Larger and obstructive calculi may require medical or surgical interventions. Medical expulsive therapy with alpha-blockers such as tamsulosin has been beneficial in stone transit.

When conservative strategies fail. Nephrologists may recommend minimally invasive treatments like ureteroscopy, percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL), and extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL). Targeted therapy may also be suggested once underlying causes such as hypercalciuria, hyperoxaluria, or uric acid imbalance have been identified.

Given that kidney stones vary widely in terms of dimensions and composition, a medical assessment supported by laboratory and diagnostic imaging procedures is the cornerstone for kidney stone care. Therefore, relying on unsupported alternative therapies like *Apamarga* root extract may not only delay the necessary intervention but may also increase the risk of complications, including infection, blockage, or renal impairment. Additionally, these remedies should only be used in moderation and under careful medical supervision.

takeaways.text:
- The claim that Apamarga root extract dissolves kidney stones in 2 to 15 days is rated as 'Mostly False' by The Healthy Indian Project.
- Scientific data does not support claims that kidney stones resolve on their own or through remedies within a few days.
- Asymptomatic kidney stones smaller than 5 mm usually take weeks to resolve, contradicting the 'days' timeline in the claim.
- Symptomatic stones ranging from 5 to 10 mm typically require treatment for a few weeks to a month.
- Apamarga root extract lacks high-quality clinical studies to support its use for all stone types or sizes.
- The root extract may not be effective for larger aggregates, staghorn calculi, cystine stones, or radiopaque infection stones.
- Experts advise that unregulated home remedies should not substitute conventional medical therapies for renal calculi due to risks of complications. <image:9801> <image:9799> <image:9800> <image:9798>

# The protective role of *Achyranthes aspera* extract against cisplatin-induced nephrotoxicity by alleviating oxidative stress, inflammation, and PANoptosis

Author links open overlay panelSong-Yi Lin a, Chia-Lin Chang d e f, Kuo-Tong Liou b h i, Yao-Kai Kao a, Yea-Hwey Wang c, Cher-Chia Chang g, [Terry B.J. ## Abstract

### Ethnopharmacological relevance

[Achyranthes aspera](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/achyranthes-aspera)*,* a widely recognized [medicinal plant](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/medicinal-plant), is used in various cultures for treating different ailments, including renal dysfunction; however, there is a lack of comprehensive understanding of its protective effects and the underlying signaling networks involved.

### Aim of the study

This study aimed to investigate the molecular mechanisms of the action of *A. aspera* by employing an integrative approach including functional and tissue imaging as well as comprehensive genomics analysis.

### Materials and methods

Cisplatin-induced [nephrotoxicity](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nephrotoxicity) is a well-established [animal model](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/animal-model) for [acute kidney injury](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/acute-kidney-failure) (AKI). In this study, we investigated the protective effects and underlying mechanisms of the action of *A. aspera* water-soluble extract (AAW) on a murine model of cisplatin-induced [AKI](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/kidney-injury). The evaluation includes measurements of [blood urea nitrogen](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/blood-urea-nitrogen) (BUN) and [serum creatinine](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/creatinine-blood-level) (SCr) levels, histology examination, and [transcriptome analysis](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/gene-expression-profiling) using [RNA sequencing](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/rna-sequencing).

### Results

In male [ICR mice](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/institute-for-cancer-research-mouse), oral administration of AAW at doses of 0.5–1.0 g/kg significantly reduced cisplatin-induced [nephrotoxicity](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nephrotoxicity). This effect included the amelioration of tubular [injury](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/injury), [renal fibrosis](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/kidney-fibrosis), and the lowering of BUN and SCr levels. AAW also effectively decreased oxidative markers, such as [malondialdehyde](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/malonaldehyde) (MDA) and [nitrotyrosine](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/3-nitrotyrosine) (NT), along with inflammation markers, including COX-2, iNOS, [NLRP3](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/nlr-family-pyrin-domain-containing-3), and pP65NFκB. Moreover, AAW administration induced a dose-dependent increase in the expression of two protective factors, [Nrf2](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transcription-factor-nrf2) and BcL2, and suppressed apoptosis, as evidenced by reduced levels of truncated [caspase 3](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/caspase-3) (t-Casp3). To explore the underlying molecular mechanisms and signaling networks, next-generation sequencing (NGS) analysis was employed. The results revealed that AAW mitigated apoptosis, [necroptosis](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/necroptosis), and PANoptosis pathways by inhibiting inflammation [signaling pathways](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/signal-transduction), such as the TNFα-, NFκB-, NETs-, and leukocyte transendothelial migration pathways. Additionally, AAW was found to enhance protective [signaling pathways](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/signal-transduction), including the cGMP/PKG-, cAMP-, AMPK-, and mTOR-dependent activation of autophagy and [mitophagy](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mitophagy) pathways. The primary bioactive compound found in AAW was identified as 20-hydroxyecdysone (0.36%).

### Conclusion

Our study demonstrates that AAW reduces cisplatin-induced nephrotoxicity. The protective effects of AAW are attributed to its modulation of multiple molecular signaling networks. Specifically, AAW downregulates genes and signaling pathways associated with [oxidative stress](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/oxidative-stress) and endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress, inflammation, and PANoptosis. Simultaneously, it upregulates genes and signaling pathways associated with cell survival, including autophagy and [mitophagy](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mitophagy) pathways.

## Introduction

Acute kidney injury (AKI) is a condition with substantial morbidity and mortality; it is marked by a rapid decline in renal filtration function. It affects approximately 12.2% of hospitalized patients, with a higher prevalence in low- and middle-income countries. Without proper treatment, AKI may be life-threatening. The mortality rate associated with AKI in adults is 23.9%, and it is even higher among those receiving kidney replacement therapy, leading to irreversible losses for both individuals and society (Guo et al., 2023).

Cisplatin-induced nephrotoxicity is a well-established animal model for AKI study (Sharp and Siskind, 2017). The development of cisplatin-induced AKI involves oxidative stress, inflammation, and mitochondrial dysfunction, leading to tissue damage and impaired renal function, evident from elevated serum creatinine (SCr) levels (Ozkok and Edelstein, 2014). Cisplatin accumulation in the kidneys causes DNA adduct formation and apoptosis in renal tubular cells. These effects reduce the renal blood flow and glomerular filtration rate and ultimately leading to AKI. Tubular injury, characterized by brush border loss, tubular dilation, and cell detachment, is an early sign of cisplatin-induced AKI. If left untreated, inflammation and fibrosis can progress to chronic kidney disease (CKD) (Chawla et al., 2014; Heung and Chawla, 2014; Palant et al., 2016).

Currently, no specific treatment exists for cisplatin-induced AKI, and ongoing research is focused on discovering new therapeutic targets. Various strategies have been proposed, such as the use of nephroprotective agents like prednisolone (Uozumi et al., 1996) and antioxidants (Darwish et al., 2018), but their effectiveness in reducing the incidence and severity of AKI is still under investigation. Further research is needed to identify novel therapeutic targets and strategies for preventing or minimizing cisplatin-induced AKI.

Achyranthes, a member of the amaranth family (Amaranthaceae), comprises both medicinal and ornamental plants. In this genus, the terms “Niuxi” or “Chaff flower” are commonly used to refer to these plants. Two important medicinal species including *A. aspera* and *A. bidentate* showing significant values in Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) were recognized as treasured herbs in Shennong's Classic of Materia Medica, particularly for their effects on the liver and kidney meridians (China Pharmacopoeia Commission, 2015).

*A. aspera* has a wide range of applications in traditional medicine, being used for treating gynecological disorders, asthma, and abdominal tumors, as well as addressing conditions like stranguria syndrome, edema, and dysuria. In Indian traditions, *A. aspera* holds cultural and medicinal significance, finding use in Ayurveda, Unani, Siddha, and Homeopathy (Perumal Samy et al., 1998). However, despite its reported effects on renal injury in rats (Aggarwal et al., 2012), its mechanisms of action and whole-genome transcriptome analysis in renal protection remain unclear.

In this study, our goal was to investigate the protective effects of *A. aspera* water extract (AAW) on AKI and understand the molecular mechanisms involved. We also compared AAW with prednisolone, a commonly used drug for AKI therapy. Our experiments included assessing renal functions, examining pathological changes, performing immunohistochemical (IHC) stains, and conducting genomic profiling in cisplatin-induced AKI mice treated with AAW. The study's findings offer insights into the potential therapeutic benefits of *A. aspera* in cisplatin-induced AKI and contribute to a better understanding of its genomic mechanisms of action.

The herbal material used in this study was the dry root of *Achyranthes aspera* L. var. rubro-dusca Hook. f. The plant material was purchased from Tianshun Ginseng Medicine & Herbal Shop in Taiwan. A voucher specimen (No. NRICM-20210510A) was deposited in the National Research Institute of Chinese Medicine, Ministry of Health and Welfare,

## AAW attenuates cisplatin-induced nephrotoxicity (renal dysfunction) in mice

To assess the potential of AAW in protecting against cisplatin-induced nephrotoxicity and its impact on renal function, AAW at doses of 0.5–1.0 g/kg body weight, or the reference drug prednisolone at a dose of 2.0 mg/kg body weight, was orally administered for five consecutive days. BUN and SCr levels were measured on day 5 to assess renal function. As illustrated in Fig. 2, cisplatin (CPT) administration (once only, on day 2) led to significant increases in BUN and SCr levels (*p* < 0.05,

## Discussion

Our study demonstrates for the first time the protective effect of *A. aspera* water extract (AAW) against cisplatin-induced AKI in mice. AAW (0.5-1.0 g/kg, p.o., once daily) significantly improved renal function and exhibited stronger renoprotective effects than those of prednisolone. We observed reduced tissue injury, inflammation, and oxidative stress as indicated by improved SCr, BUN, MDA, and NT levels. These effects were associated with the activation of cell survival signaling pathways,

 2025, Kidney International

 Show abstract

 Podocytes can undergo PANoptosis (apoptosis, pyroptosis, and necroptosis). Diabetic kidney disease (DKD) is the leading cause of kidney failure, and podocyte loss is a major event leading to the progression of DKD. Here, we compared single cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data between three normal and three DKD human kidney samples and found a significant increase of *TNFSF10* and *TNFRSF10B* expression in podocytes of patients with DKD. Tumor necrosis factor (TNF)-related apoptosis–inducing ligand (TRAIL), coded by *TNFSF10*, belongs to the TNF superfamily members and *TNFRSF10B* codes for death receptor 5 (DR5). We confirmed that expression of TRAIL and DR5 increased in podocytes of patients with DKD and correlated with the severity of DKD. *In vitro*, TNF-α stimulated TRAIL and DR5 expression in cultured human podocytes. Silence of TRAIL or DR5 by small interfering RNA alleviated TNF-α-stimulated podocytes PANoptosis, while overexpression of TRAIL, treatment with recombinant human TRAIL (rh-TRAIL) or the DR5 activator (Bioymifi) enhanced podocytes PANoptosis. *In vivo*, podocyte-specific deletion of *TNFSF10* or *TNFRSF10B* alleviated podocyte and glomerular injury in high fat diet and streptozotocin-induced obese diabetic mice and was associated with decreased podocyte PANoptosis. Cisplatin-induced [nephrotoxicity](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nephrotoxicity) is a well-established [animal model](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/animal-model) for [acute kidney injury](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/acute-kidney-failure) (AKI). In this study, we investigated the protective effects and underlying mechanisms of the action of *A. aspera* water-soluble extract (AAW) on a murine model of cisplatin-induced [AKI](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/kidney-injury). The evaluation includes measurements of [blood urea nitrogen](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/blood-urea-nitrogen) (BUN) and [serum creatinine](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/creatinine-blood-level) (SCr) levels, histology examination, and [transcriptome analysis](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/gene-expression-profiling) using [RNA sequencing](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/rna-sequencing).

### Results

In male [ICR mice](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/institute-for-cancer-research-mouse), oral administration of AAW at doses of 0.5–1.0 g/kg significantly reduced cisplatin-induced [nephrotoxicity](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nephrotoxicity). This effect included the amelioration of tubular [injury](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/injury), [renal fibrosis](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/kidney-fibrosis), and the lowering of BUN and SCr levels. AAW also effectively decreased oxidative markers, such as [malondialdehyde](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/malonaldehyde) (MDA) and [nitrotyrosine](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/3-nitrotyrosine) (NT), along with inflammation markers, including COX-2, iNOS, [NLRP3](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/nlr-family-pyrin-domain-containing-3), and pP65NFκB. Moreover, AAW administration induced a dose-dependent increase in the expression of two protective factors, [Nrf2](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transcription-factor-nrf2) and BcL2, and suppressed apoptosis, as evidenced by reduced levels of truncated [caspase 3](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/caspase-3) (t-Casp3). To explore the underlying molecular mechanisms and signaling networks, next-generation sequencing (NGS) analysis was employed. The results revealed that AAW mitigated apoptosis, [necroptosis](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/necroptosis), and PANoptosis pathways by inhibiting inflammation [signaling pathways](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/signal-transduction), such as the TNFα-, NFκB-, NETs-, and leukocyte transendothelial migration pathways. Additionally, AAW was found to enhance protective [signaling pathways](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/signal-transduction), including the cGMP/PKG-, cAMP-, AMPK-, and mTOR-dependent activation of autophagy and [mitophagy](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mitophagy) pathways. The primary bioactive compound found in AAW was identified as 20-hydroxyecdysone (0.36%).

### Conclusion

Our study demonstrates that AAW reduces cisplatin-induced nephrotoxicity. The protective effects of AAW are attributed to its modulation of multiple molecular signaling networks. Specifically, AAW downregulates genes and signaling pathways associated with [oxidative stress](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/oxidative-stress) and endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress, inflammation, and PANoptosis. Simultaneously, it upregulates genes and signaling pathways associated with cell survival, including autophagy and [mitophagy](/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mitophagy) pathways.

## Introduction

Acute kidney injury (AKI) is a condition with substantial morbidity and mortality; it is marked by a rapid decline in renal filtration function. It affects approximately 12.2% of hospitalized patients, with a higher prevalence in low- and middle-income countries. Without proper treatment, AKI may be life-threatening. The mortality rate associated with AKI in adults is 23.9%, and it is even higher among those receiving kidney replacement therapy, leading to irreversible losses for both individuals and society (Guo et al., 2023).

Cisplatin-induced nephrotoxicity is a well-established animal model for AKI study (Sharp and Siskind, 2017). The development of cisplatin-induced AKI involves oxidative stress, inflammation, and mitochondrial dysfunction, leading to tissue damage and impaired renal function, evident from elevated serum creatinine (SCr) levels (Ozkok and Edelstein, 2014). Cisplatin accumulation in the kidneys causes DNA adduct formation and apoptosis in renal tubular cells. These effects reduce the renal blood flow and glomerular filtration rate and ultimately leading to AKI. Tubular injury, characterized by brush border loss, tubular dilation, and cell detachment, is an early sign of cisplatin-induced AKI. If left untreated, inflammation and fibrosis can progress to chronic kidney disease (CKD) (Chawla et al., 2014; Heung and Chawla, 2014; Palant et al., 2016).

Currently, no specific treatment exists for cisplatin-induced AKI, and ongoing research is focused on discovering new therapeutic targets. Various strategies have been proposed, such as the use of nephroprotective agents like prednisolone (Uozumi et al., 1996) and antioxidants (Darwish et al., 2018), but their effectiveness in reducing the incidence and severity of AKI is still under investigation. Further research is needed to identify novel therapeutic targets and strategies for preventing or minimizing cisplatin-induced AKI.

Achyranthes, a member of the amaranth family (Amaranthaceae), comprises both medicinal and ornamental plants. In this genus, the terms “Niuxi” or “Chaff flower” are commonly used to refer to these plants. Two important medicinal species including *A. aspera* and *A. bidentate* showing significant values in Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) were recognized as treasured herbs in Shennong's Classic of Materia Medica, particularly for their effects on the liver and kidney meridians (China Pharmacopoeia Commission, 2015).

*A. aspera* has a wide range of applications in traditional medicine, being used for treating gynecological disorders, asthma, and abdominal tumors, as well as addressing conditions like stranguria syndrome, edema, and dysuria. In Indian traditions, *A. aspera* holds cultural and medicinal significance, finding use in Ayurveda, Unani, Siddha, and Homeopathy (Perumal Samy et al., 1998). However, despite its reported effects on renal injury in rats (Aggarwal et al., 2012), its mechanisms of action and whole-genome transcriptome analysis in renal protection remain unclear.

In this study, our goal was to investigate the protective effects of *A. aspera* water extract (AAW) on AKI and understand the molecular mechanisms involved. We also compared AAW with prednisolone, a commonly used drug for AKI therapy. Our experiments included assessing renal functions, examining pathological changes, performing immunohistochemical (IHC) stains, and conducting genomic profiling in cisplatin-induced AKI mice treated with AAW. The study's findings offer insights into the potential therapeutic benefits of *A. aspera* in cisplatin-induced AKI and contribute to a better understanding of its genomic mechanisms of action.

## Plant material

The herbal material used in this study was the dry root of *Achyranthes aspera* L. var. rubro-dusca Hook. f. It was identified by Dr. Chia-Ching Liao, one of the corresponding authors and curator of the Herbarium of the National Research Institute of Chinese Medicine. The plant material was purchased from Tianshun Ginseng Medicine & Herbal Shop in Taiwan. A voucher specimen (No. NRICM-20210510A) was deposited in the National Research Institute of Chinese Medicine, Ministry of Health and Welfare,

## AAW attenuates cisplatin-induced nephrotoxicity (renal dysfunction) in mice

To assess the potential of AAW in protecting against cisplatin-induced nephrotoxicity and its impact on renal function, AAW at doses of 0.5–1.0 g/kg body weight, or the reference drug prednisolone at a dose of 2.0 mg/kg body weight, was orally administered for five consecutive days. BUN and SCr levels were measured on day 5 to assess renal function. As illustrated in Fig. 2, cisplatin (CPT) administration (once only, on day 2) led to significant increases in BUN and SCr levels (*p* < 0.05,

## Discussion

Our study demonstrates for the first time the protective effect of *A. aspera* water extract (AAW) against cisplatin-induced AKI in mice. AAW (0.5-1.0 g/kg, p.o., once daily) significantly improved renal function and exhibited stronger renoprotective effects than those of prednisolone. We observed reduced tissue injury, inflammation, and oxidative stress as indicated by improved SCr, BUN, MDA, and NT levels. These effects were associated with the activation of cell survival signaling pathways,

## Experimental studies of Achyranthes aspera (L) preventing nephrotoxicity induced by lead in albino rats

*Jayakumar, T; Sridhar, MP; Bharathprasad, TR; Ilayaraja, M; Govindasamy, S; Balasubramanian, MP*

HERO ID

| **HERO ID** | 483869 |
| **In Press** | No |
| **Year** | 2009 |
| **Title** | Experimental studies of Achyranthes aspera (L) preventing nephrotoxicity induced by lead in albino rats |
| **Authors** | Jayakumar, T; Sridhar, MP; Bharathprasad, TR; Ilayaraja, M; Govindasamy, S; Balasubramanian, MP |
| **Journal** | Journal of Health Science |
| **Volume** | 55 |
| **Issue** | 5 |
| **Page Numbers** | 701-708 |
| **Abstract** | The present study was designed to evaluate the nephroprotective role of methanolic extract of Achyranthes aspera (A. aspera) an important herb in the Indian system of medicine against lead acetate-induced nephrotoxicity in rats. Toxicity was induced in male albino rats (Wistar strain) by administering lead acetate (0.2%) in drinking water for 6 weeks, followed by extract of A. aspera (200 mg/kg body weight). Changes in kidney weights encountered upon lead administration improved after extract with A. aspera. Lead damage to the urine was evident from increase in the activity of gamma-glutamyltranspeptidase (gamma-GT), Cathespin D, alkaline phosphatase (ALP), acid phosphatase (ACP), beta-glucuronidase lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) and N-acetyl-beta-D-glucosaminidase (NAG) in urine along with some urinary constituents (urea, uric acid, creatinine, protein and phosphorous). The effects of lead were also studied in kidney (gamma-GT,beta-glucuronidase, NAG, Cathespin D and LDH) and showed a decline upon extract administration. Increased activities of urinary enzymes were accompanied by increase in the urinary constituents. Treatment with methanolic extract of A. aspera after lead induction completely ameliorated the lead-induced renal damage. |

The effects of lead were also studied in kidney (gamma-GT,beta-glucuronidase, NAG, Cathespin D and LDH) and showed a decline upon extract administration. Increased activities of urinary enzymes were accompanied by increase in the urinary constituents. Treatment with methanolic extract of A. aspera after lead induction completely ameliorated the lead-induced renal damage. |

takeaways.text:
- The study evaluated the nephroprotective role of methanolic extract of Achyranthes aspera.
- The research focused on preventing nephrotoxicity induced by lead in albino rats.
- Toxicity was induced in male albino rats by administering lead acetate in drinking water for 6 weeks.
- Changes in kidney weights encountered upon lead administration improved after extract with A. aspera.
- Treatment with methanolic extract of A. aspera after lead induction completely ameliorated the lead-induced renal damage.
- Lead damage to the urine was evident from increase in the activity of enzymes such as gamma-glutamyltranspeptidase.
- Achyranthes aspera is described as an important herb in the Indian system of medicine.

# Fact Check: Can a herbal concoction help dissolve kidney stones in just 8 days?

Fact Checked By [Dr. Saumya Saluja](https://www.thip.media/author/dr-saumya-saluja/)

[shows\_reviwer]

Published on:

December 26, 2025

Last updated on: December 25, 2025

Share

[Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.thip.media%2Fhealth-news-fact-check%2Ffact-check-can-a-herbal-concoction-help-dissolve-kidney-stones-in-just-8-days%2F134958%2F "Facebook")[X](https://x.com/intent/post?text=Fact+Check%3A+Can+a+herbal+concoction+help+dissolve+kidney+stones+in+just+8+days%3F&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.thip.media%2Fhealth-news-fact-check%2Ffact-check-can-a-herbal-concoction-help-dissolve-kidney-stones-in-just-8-days%2F134958%2F&via=THIP+Media "X")[WhatsApp](https://api.whatsapp.com/send?text=Fact+Check%3A+Can+a+herbal+concoction+help+dissolve+kidney+stones+in+just+8+days%3F %0A%0A https://www.thip.media/health-news-fact-check/fact-check-can-a-herbal-concoction-help-dissolve-kidney-stones-in-just-8-days/134958/ "WhatsApp")[Linkedin](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https://www.thip.media/health-news-fact-check/fact-check-can-a-herbal-concoction-help-dissolve-kidney-stones-in-just-8-days/134958/&title=Fact+Check%3A+Can+a+herbal+concoction+help+dissolve+kidney+stones+in+just+8+days%3F "Linkedin")[Copy URL](https://www.thip.media/health-news-fact-check/fact-check-can-a-herbal-concoction-help-dissolve-kidney-stones-in-just-8-days/134958/ "Copy URL")

### Quick Take

An Instagram post suggests that a herbal concoction can remove kidney stones in just 8 days. After analysing the notion, we found it false.

false rating <image:9932> 
### The Claim

A well-circulated [Instagram post](https://www.instagram.com/p/DLKD1cpTWFb/) with the caption “पथरी बाहर kidney Stone Remedy” promotes the consumption of a combination of fennel, carom and cumin seeds for the dissolution of kidney stones of any size.

dissolve kidney stones  <image:9934> 
### Fact Check

## How do renal (stones) calculi develop? Who is more prone to developing them?

When components such as calcium, oxalate, and uric acid precipitate and aggregate together into solid or firm lumps in the renal urinary or Bowman’s space, they are known as kidney stones, [medically known as renal calculi](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK442014/). Despite the elevated [incidence of renal aggregates](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38155376/), the following elements could [elevate an individual’s risk](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34192414/):

* Disturbed urine environment – primarily induced by dehydration
* Familial inheritance and gastrointestinal issues
* weight gain and specific prescription medications
* Consumption of large amounts of animal protein, salt, or oxalate-rich meals

Because stones in the kidney sometimes serve as indicators of underlying health issues, analysing urine chemistry and kidney stone composition may provide a window into diagnosing them. Following the completion of the evaluation, the condition is appropriately addressed.

## Are kidney stones effectively dissolved by fenugreek, cumin, and carom seeds?

No, not exactly. While the herbal concoction formulated with fenugreek (methi), cumin (jeera), and carom (ajwain) seeds has been promoted as a kidney stone elimination agent, its significance is confined to social media platforms. Given that the three spices are under consideration for a very uncomfortable condition, they should be carefully examined.

### Fenugreek (*methi*) seeds

We begin [with fenugreek seeds](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17582593/) (*Trigonella foenum-graecum*), a well-known folk remedy with antioxidant and anti-lithogenic properties. Methi seeds may prevent kidney stones from forming rather than dissolving them by reducing the deposition of calcium-oxalate renal crystals. Since these results are only supported by animal research, large-scale human trials are required to validate fenugreek as a primary intervention for renal stone dissolution.

### Carom (*ajwain*) seeds

The [carom seeds, or *Trachyspermum ammi*](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3358968/), come next. The carom seed extract is [under examination due to its antiurolithic activity](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36867274/), which includes diuretic effects and suppression of calcium-oxalate crystal aggregation. Additionally, carom seeds may facilitate passage or prevent the formation of stones. However, these potential advantages are limited to preliminary research and rat models. Therefore, additional solid medical evidence that carom seeds can remove kidney stones in humans is still required.

### Cumin (*jeera*) seeds

Finally, we explore the potential medicinal benefits of cumin seeds. Although [*Cuminum cyminum* seed extracts](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/378797140_Evaluation_of_anti-urolithiatic_activity_of_seed_extract_of_Cuminum_cyminum?__cf_chl_tk=f_Bkq6475sSsUQ0NYJ3SRsNEmYSeowbkM81Nox5Nv2Y-1766672699-1.0.1.1-ockK2.jfSm._muBaTUZ_DA_pYirrMbP8jqZvFHy5ax4) may reduce the nucleation and development of calcium oxalate crystals, their effects remain restricted to preclinical investigations. Therefore, the ingredient’s consumption for renal stone dissolution requires more extensive and thorough testing.

![Dr Sharad Malhotra, Gastroenterologist](https://static.thip.media/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/12160555/Sharad-Malhotra-edited.jpg)

**Dr. Sharad Malhotra, HOD Gastroenterology, Hepatology & Therapeutic Endoscopy, Aakash Healthcare, New Delhi**, cautions against using home remedies as a long-term [treatment](https://www.thip.media/treatment/ "List of Treatment Guides") for kidney stones, even though they can be beneficial for digestive or urinary health. He further emphasised that there is not sufficient evidence that these measures may eliminate or manage existing kidney calculi. It is never advisable to use these unsubstantiated substitutes for adequate medical care.

Although fenugreek, cumin, and carom seeds have promising anti-lithogenic or preventative qualities, there is no definitive evidence that they can efficiently dissolve kidney stones in humans, either alone or in combination. They might enhance urinary health or help avoid the formation of stones, but they shouldn’t be the [primary course of action for existing renal calculi](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9959749/).

## Is it safe to depend on online cures that claim kidney stone eradication in eight days?

No, it is not advised to utilise [unregulated online alternatives](https://www.thip.media/health-news-fact-check/fact-check-can-ajwain-water-with-sugar-help-dissolve-kidney-stones/129736/), particularly those that promote kidney stone dissolution timelines. Despite their attractive packaging and widespread use as [natural, chemical-free substitutes](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37644348/), such [unsubstantiated remedies](https://www.thip.media/health-news-fact-check/fact-check-can-a-herbal-infusion-dissolve-kidney-stones-in-a-few-seconds/134485/) lack scientific backing. Moreover, [these unverified therapies](https://www.thip.media/health-news-fact-check/fact-check-does-a-papaya-seed-based-recipe-dissolve-kidney-stones/133449/) lack standardisation in terms of dosage, safety, and efficacy.

![Dr. Archana Daftardar](https://static.thip.media/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/03194136/IMG-20250731-WA0014-1.jpg)

This is further backed by the fact that there are many myths regarding the treatment and avoidance of renal stones, asserts **Dr. Archana Daftardar, Consultant Nephrologist and transplant and hypertension specialist at Renova Hospitals in Kompally, Hyderabad**. Unproven natural remedies might make the condition worse rather than better. Hence, it is recommended to consult a licensed Ayurvedic or allopathic doctor for timely evaluation and correct intervention.

While some online options may provide symptomatic relief and may help reduce stone formation risk or assist passage over time, their role is limited to being used as an adjunct and not a primary treatment option for kidney stones. Therefore, competent physician evaluation and [evidence-based therapy](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40891477/) remain the safest course of action, especially for stones that cause discomfort or complications.

## What do medical professionals suggest for managing kidney stones?

Usually, [renal stones undergo therapy](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40891477/) as per their chemical composition, anatomical location, and geometric characteristics. Despite the prevalence of asymptomatic stones, [symptomatic calculi can be managed by](https://www.erknet.org/fileadmin/files/user_upload/Medical_management_of_kidney_stones_AUA_guideline..pdf):

* Initial analgesic therapy – this is the first line of treatment – is utilised for pain control. [Medical professionals might suggest](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3313741/) nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) or opioids depending on how uncomfortable a patient is.
* Renal calculi smaller than 5 mm: Such patients may be managed via medicines and urged to drink more water. This causes the ureter’s muscles to relax, which facilitates the passage of stones.
* Obstructive larger calculi: These may require surgical removal if they are impeding urine flow, causing infection, or creating ongoing discomfort. Experts typically use extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL), ureteroscopy, or percutaneous nephrolithotomy.

Clinicians focus on preventing recurrence after the first painful episode. This would include [nutritional guidance based on stone composition](https://www.thip.media/diet-and-nutrition/what-are-the-foods-to-prevent-kidney-stones/69165/) and metabolic assessment. Urinalysis and blood testing may help identify any underlying medical conditions. 
 
Both the immediate discomfort and the potential risk of kidney stones are addressed by the multimodal approach.

# Chirchita (Apamarg/Prickly Chaff): Ayurvedic Benefits, Uses, and Side Effects

Jan 27, 2025

0 comments
 <image:9945> 
## What is Chirchita (Apamarga Plant)?

Prickly Chaff Flower, known as Chirchita (Achyranthes aspera) is an Ayurvedic medicinal herbs having plethora of health benefits. So it grows in tropical and sub-tropical areas, and has extremely powerful healing properties. Chirchita is known for supporting digestion, detoxifying the body, and promoting overall well-being.

It is mainly used for digestive health, as it alleviates constipation, indigestion, and bloating by improving gut function. It is a natural blood purifier as well, helping the body eliminate toxins and improve skin quality. However, the Antioxidants in Chirchita make it very good for joints and muscles as it reduces swelling and pain, thus helpful in Arthritis and Relieved when used on joints. 

### Synonyms: Apamarga, Prickly Chaff, and Regional Names

Chirchita has different names in various cultures and languages. It is known as Apamarga in Sanskrit and Chirchita in Hindi. Also, it is also known as Devil's Horsewhip in English.

### Botanical Information and Classification

* - Scientific Name: Achyranthes aspera
* - Family: Amaranthaceae
* - Genus: Achyranthes

## Chirchita Plant Overview

Chirchita is an erect, woody herb that grows up to 1–2 meters in height. It has small greenish flowers, rough stems, and characteristic spiny seeds that stick to clothing and animal fur.

### Habitat: Where It Grows Naturally

It grows abundantly in tropical and subtropical areas. Also, it can be commonly found in India, Nepal, Sri Lanka, and parts of Africa. It is found to thrive in waste places, roadsides, and cultivated lands.

Ayurveda uses all the parts of the Chirchita plant, including:

* - Leaves
* - Roots
* - Seeds
* - Stem

## Ayurvedic Significance of Chirchita

One of the most medicinal plants mentioned in the Charaka Saṁhitā and Sushruda Saṁhitā is chirchita, which can bring genuine healing to a number of conditions in human beings. It has numerous healing properties which have proven to be beneficial since a long time. 

Dosha Balancing Properties of Chirchita: Vata, Pitta, and Kapha

* - Balances Vata and Kapha doshas
* - Helps in Pitta disorders when used in moderation

## Chirchita Plant Benefits

* - Anti-inflammatory
* - Diuretic
* - Digestive stimulant
* - Blood purifier
* - Antimicrobial

## Health Benefits of Chirchita

#### 1. Digestive Health
 <image:9943> 
Chirchita is also known to digest the food easily as it induces appetite, prevents bloating, and treats constipation.

#### 2. Skin Care

Since Chirchita contains antimicrobial properties, it is also used in the treatment of infections on the skin, wounds, and acne.

#### 3. Kidney and Urinary Tract Health

Apamarga is used as a diuretic to cleanse the kidneys and avoid infection of the urinary tract.

#### 4. Blood Purification

It purifies the blood and cleanses the skin by removing the toxins.

#### 5. Role in Weight Management

Chirchita increases the metabolism and facilitates the loss of fats, hence useful in managing weight.

### Other Therapeutic Uses

* - Relieves joint pain and inflammation
* - Treats respiratory issues like asthma and cough

## How to Use Chirchita (Apamarga Plant)

### Forms: Powder, Juice, Decoction, and Oil

* - Powder: Used for digestion and detoxification
* - Juice: Beneficial for skin and kidney health
* - Decoction: Helps with respiratory and urinary issues
* - Oil: Applied externally for wounds and joint pain

### Recipes: DIY Remedies Using Chirchita

1. - For Digestive Health: Mix 1 tsp Chirchita powder with warm water and honey.
2. - For Skin Issues: Apply Chirchita paste on acne and wounds.
3. - For Kidney Health: Drink Chirchita decoction once daily.

### Dosage Recommendations

* - Powder: 1–3 grams per day
* - Juice: 10–20 ml per day
* - Decoction: 30–50 ml per day

## Apamarga Plant Benefits in Ayurveda

The anti-inflammatory properties help to lessen swelling and pain.

### Benefits for Diabetes and Metabolic Disorders
 <image:9944> 
Chirchita helps regulate blood sugar levels, making it useful for diabetics.Chirchita helps in curing the metabolic disorders and regulating blood sugar levels, Thus it is considered useful for the purpose of diabetes.

### Wound Healing and Antimicrobial Properties

It Improve wound healing and protects against bacterial infection. Side Effects and Precautions

## Side Effects and Precautions

### Who Should Avoid Chirchita?

* - Pregnant and breastfeeding women
* - Individuals with severe acidity or gastritis

### Potential Interactions with Medications

* - May interact with diuretics and blood sugar medications

### Safe Dosage Guidelines

Always consult an Ayurvedic practitioner before using Chirchita extensively.

## Scientific Research on Chirchita

Recent studies validate Chirchita’s antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties.

### Nutritional Composition of Chirchita

Rich in alkaloids, flavonoids, and saponins, contributing to its medicinal benefits.

### Comparison with Similar Medicinal Plants

While both are blood purifiers, Chirchita is also a diuretic and digestive stimulant.

#### Unique Features of Apamarga

Unlike other herbs, Chirchita is beneficial for kidney health and weight management.

## Chirchita in Modern Herbal Medicine

Leading Ayurvedic brands include Chirchita in herbal formulations for digestion, detoxification, and immunity.

### Growing Trend of Chirchita in Natural Health Products

With increasing awareness of Ayurvedic remedies, Chirchita is gaining popularity in herbal teas, supplements, and skincare products.

## FAQs About Chirchita and Apamarga Plant

#### Q: Can Chirchita help with kidney stones?

A: Yes, its diuretic properties help flush out kidney stones naturally.

#### Q: Is Chirchita safe for daily use?

A: When taken in moderate amounts, it is safe. Always consult a healthcare provider.

## Conclusion

Chirchita, also called Apamarga, is an extremely potent Ayurvedic herb that is helpful for digestion, skin care, and kidney health. It can be added to both traditional and modern medicine to add value to daily wellness practices. Try adding Chirchita to your life through herbal teas, powders, or topical applications for a holistic approach to health.

# Chirchita (Apamarg/Prickly Chaff): Ayurvedic Benefits, Uses, and Side Effects

Jan 27, 2025

0 comments
 <image:9945> 
## What is Chirchita (Apamarga Plant)?

Prickly Chaff Flower, known as Chirchita (Achyranthes aspera) is an Ayurvedic medicinal herbs having plethora of health benefits. So it grows in tropical and sub-tropical areas, and has extremely powerful healing properties. Chirchita is known for supporting digestion, detoxifying the body, and promoting overall well-being.

It is mainly used for digestive health, as it alleviates constipation, indigestion, and bloating by improving gut function. It is a natural blood purifier as well, helping the body eliminate toxins and improve skin quality. However, the Antioxidants in Chirchita make it very good for joints and muscles as it reduces swelling and pain, thus helpful in Arthritis and Relieved when used on joints. 

### Synonyms: Apamarga, Prickly Chaff, and Regional Names

Chirchita has different names in various cultures and languages. It is known as Apamarga in Sanskrit and Chirchita in Hindi. Also, it is also known as Devil's Horsewhip in English.

### Botanical Information and Classification

* - Scientific Name: Achyranthes aspera
* - Family: Amaranthaceae
* - Genus: Achyranthes

## Chirchita Plant Overview

Chirchita is an erect, woody herb that grows up to 1–2 meters in height. It has small greenish flowers, rough stems, and characteristic spiny seeds that stick to clothing and animal fur.

### Habitat: Where It Grows Naturally

It grows abundantly in tropical and subtropical areas. Also, it can be commonly found in India, Nepal, Sri Lanka, and parts of Africa. It is found to thrive in waste places, roadsides, and cultivated lands.

Ayurveda uses all the parts of the Chirchita plant, including:

* - Leaves
* - Roots
* - Seeds
* - Stem

## Ayurvedic Significance of Chirchita

One of the most medicinal plants mentioned in the Charaka Saṁhitā and Sushruda Saṁhitā is chirchita, which can bring genuine healing to a number of conditions in human beings. It has numerous healing properties which have proven to be beneficial since a long time. 

Dosha Balancing Properties of Chirchita: Vata, Pitta, and Kapha

* - Balances Vata and Kapha doshas
* - Helps in Pitta disorders when used in moderation

## Chirchita Plant Benefits

* - Anti-inflammatory
* - Diuretic
* - Digestive stimulant
* - Blood purifier
* - Antimicrobial

## Health Benefits of Chirchita

#### 1. Digestive Health
 <image:9943> 
Chirchita is also known to digest the food easily as it induces appetite, prevents bloating, and treats constipation.

#### 2. Skin Care

Since Chirchita contains antimicrobial properties, it is also used in the treatment of infections on the skin, wounds, and acne.

#### 3. Kidney and Urinary Tract Health

Apamarga is used as a diuretic to cleanse the kidneys and avoid infection of the urinary tract.

#### 4. Blood Purification

It purifies the blood and cleanses the skin by removing the toxins.

#### 5. Role in Weight Management

Chirchita increases the metabolism and facilitates the loss of fats, hence useful in managing weight.

### Other Therapeutic Uses

* - Relieves joint pain and inflammation
* - Treats respiratory issues like asthma and cough

## How to Use Chirchita (Apamarga Plant)

### Forms: Powder, Juice, Decoction, and Oil

* - Powder: Used for digestion and detoxification
* - Juice: Beneficial for skin and kidney health
* - Decoction: Helps with respiratory and urinary issues
* - Oil: Applied externally for wounds and joint pain

### Recipes: DIY Remedies Using Chirchita

1. - For Digestive Health: Mix 1 tsp Chirchita powder with warm water and honey.
2. - For Skin Issues: Apply Chirchita paste on acne and wounds.
3. - For Kidney Health: Drink Chirchita decoction once daily.

### Dosage Recommendations

* - Powder: 1–3 grams per day
* - Juice: 10–20 ml per day
* - Decoction: 30–50 ml per day

## Apamarga Plant Benefits in Ayurveda

The anti-inflammatory properties help to lessen swelling and pain.

### Benefits for Diabetes and Metabolic Disorders
 <image:9944> 
Chirchita helps regulate blood sugar levels, making it useful for diabetics.Chirchita helps in curing the metabolic disorders and regulating blood sugar levels, Thus it is considered useful for the purpose of diabetes.

### Wound Healing and Antimicrobial Properties

It Improve wound healing and protects against bacterial infection. Side Effects and Precautions

## Side Effects and Precautions

### Who Should Avoid Chirchita?

* - Pregnant and breastfeeding women
* - Individuals with severe acidity or gastritis

### Potential Interactions with Medications

* - May interact with diuretics and blood sugar medications

### Safe Dosage Guidelines

Always consult an Ayurvedic practitioner before using Chirchita extensively.

## Scientific Research on Chirchita

Recent studies validate Chirchita’s antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties.

### Nutritional Composition of Chirchita

Rich in alkaloids, flavonoids, and saponins, contributing to its medicinal benefits.

### Comparison with Similar Medicinal Plants

While both are blood purifiers, Chirchita is also a diuretic and digestive stimulant.

#### Unique Features of Apamarga

Unlike other herbs, Chirchita is beneficial for kidney health and weight management.

## Chirchita in Modern Herbal Medicine

Leading Ayurvedic brands include Chirchita in herbal formulations for digestion, detoxification, and immunity.

### Growing Trend of Chirchita in Natural Health Products

With increasing awareness of Ayurvedic remedies, Chirchita is gaining popularity in herbal teas, supplements, and skincare products.

## FAQs About Chirchita and Apamarga Plant

#### Q: Can Chirchita help with kidney stones?

A: Yes, its diuretic properties help flush out kidney stones naturally.

#### Q: Is Chirchita safe for daily use?

A: When taken in moderate amounts, it is safe. Always consult a healthcare provider.

## Conclusion

Chirchita, also called Apamarga, is an extremely potent Ayurvedic herb that is helpful for digestion, skin care, and kidney health. It can be added to both traditional and modern medicine to add value to daily wellness practices. Try adding Chirchita to your life through herbal teas, powders, or topical applications for a holistic approach to health.

Task:
Search for the specific claim: 'Apamarga root extract, consumed on an empty stomach, can dissolve kidney stones of any size and completely clear them within 10–15 days.' Verify the medical accuracy of this statement regarding Achyranthes aspera (Apamarga) for kidney stones. Identify if there are any known health warnings or debunking articles. Prior session context:
Accepted evidence:
- Source: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22439435/
  Summary: - The study titled 'Preventive and curative effects of Achyranthes aspera Linn. extract in experimentally induced nephrolithiasis' was published in the Indian Journal of Experimental Biology in March 2012.
- Researchers evaluated the efficacy of Achyranthes aspera in preventing and reducing the growth of calcium oxalate stones in an ethylene glycol induced nephrolithiatic model.
- Hyperoxaluria was induced in rats using ethylene glycol and ammonium chloride for 15 days in the experiment.
- Aqueous extract of A. aspera was administered at dosages of 500 and 1000 mg/kg body weight during the study.
- Levels of renal injury markers lactate dehydrogenase and alkaline phosphatase were normalized upon administration of the extract.
- Concurrent treatment reduced changes in the architecture of renal tissue and decreased the size of crystals.
- The study indicated that Achyranthes aspera had an ability to maintain renal functioning and reduced renal injury.
- Source: https://japsonline.com/abstract.php?article_id=4032&sts=2
  Summary: - According to Ayurvedic literature, the roots and leaves of Achyranthes aspera have antilithiatic and diuretic activity.
- This study investigated the saponin-rich fraction from the methanolic extract of the aeriform (aerial) portions of Achyranthes aspera, not the roots.
- The research found that the saponin-rich fraction of Achyranthes aspera efficiently dissolves calcium oxalate (CaOx) renal stones in Wistar rats.
- Treatment with the saponin-rich fraction reduced urinary excretion of urea, uric acid, and calcium in ethylene glycol-induced urolithiasis models.
- The extract demonstrated the ability to bridge the nucleation and aggregation of calcium oxalate crystals in vitro.
- Histopathological analysis showed that the extract reduced CaOx deposition in renal tissue and minimized tubular dilatation.
- The study utilized an ethylene glycol-induced urolithiasis model in male Wistar rats to assess anti-urolithiatic activity. <image:9790>  <image:9789>  <image:9791>  <image:9791> - Source: https://patents.google.com/patent/WO2012127498A9/en
  Summary: - The patent discloses a herbal composition for kidney stone treatment containing extracts from five parts of Achyranthes aspera (Apamarg/Aghada), specifically including the root.
- The specific active ingredient derived from Achyranthes aspera in the composition is referred to as Apamargkshar, prepared by burning the dried plant parts to ash and processing with water.
- In the 'Herbmed Plus' formulation, Apamargkshar is included in an amount of approximately 15-85% of the total composition.
- A randomized clinical trial (Litho trial) involving 162 completed patients showed that the group treated with Herbmed Plus (containing Apamargkshar) achieved 90.7% complete stone clearance compared to 57.4% in the control group.
- Clinical case studies within the patent report that patients treated with the formulation containing Achyranthes aspera extracts experienced reductions in kidney stone surface area ranging from 4% to 67% or complete dissolution after one month.
- Clinical data indicates an average pain score of 0.45 for patients treated with the Achyranthes aspera containing formulation compared to 1.15 for the control group.
- A trial for stent dysuria reported that patients receiving the herbal composition (H group) showed lower average symptom scores, such as 2.5 for dysuria, compared to the group receiving anticholinergic medication (7.2). <image:9767>  <image:9768>  <image:9766>  <image:9769> - Source: https://www.thip.media/health-news-fact-check/fact-check-can-apamarga-root-extract-effectively-dissolve-kidney-stones/132405/
  Summary: - The claim that Apamarga root extract dissolves kidney stones in 2 to 15 days is rated as 'Mostly False' by The Healthy Indian Project.
- Scientific data does not support claims that kidney stones resolve on their own or through remedies within a few days.
- Asymptomatic kidney stones smaller than 5 mm usually take weeks to resolve, contradicting the 'days' timeline in the claim.
- Symptomatic stones ranging from 5 to 10 mm typically require treatment for a few weeks to a month.
- Apamarga root extract lacks high-quality clinical studies to support its use for all stone types or sizes.
- The root extract may not be effective for larger aggregates, staghorn calculi, cystine stones, or radiopaque infection stones.
- Experts advise that unregulated home remedies should not substitute conventional medical therapies for renal calculi due to risks of complications. <image:9801>  <image:9799>  <image:9800>  <image:9798> - Source: https://www.news18.com/lifestyle/biharthis-plant-can-even-break-down-25mm-kidney-stones-naturally-heres-how-to-use-it-at-home-ws-abkl-9292186.html
  Summary: Consuming just 5 to 10 grams of Apamarg throughout the day can eliminate stones up to 20 to 25 mm in size. The key is to consume it following ...
- Source: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0378874123009650
  Summary: - Achyranthes aspera is a widely recognized medicinal plant used in various cultures for treating different ailments, including renal dysfunction.
- The study investigated the protective effects of Achyranthes aspera water-soluble extract (AAW) on a murine model of cisplatin-induced acute kidney injury (AKI).
- Oral administration of AAW at doses of 0.5–1.0 g/kg significantly reduced cisplatin-induced nephrotoxicity in male ICR mice.
- AAW treatment ameliorated tubular injury and renal fibrosis while lowering blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and serum creatinine (SCr) levels.
- AAW effectively decreased oxidative markers such as malondialdehyde (MDA) and nitrotyrosine (NT) along with inflammation markers including COX-2, iNOS, and NLRP3.
- AAW mitigated apoptosis, necroptosis, and PANoptosis pathways by inhibiting inflammation signaling pathways such as TNFα-, NFκB-, NETs-, and leukocyte transendothelial migration pathways.
- The study demonstrates that AAW upregulates genes and signaling pathways associated with cell survival, including autophagy and mitophagy pathways. <image:9868> - Source: https://hero.epa.gov/reference/483869/
  Summary: - The study evaluated the nephroprotective role of methanolic extract of Achyranthes aspera.
- The research focused on preventing nephrotoxicity induced by lead in albino rats.
- Toxicity was induced in male albino rats by administering lead acetate in drinking water for 6 weeks.
- Changes in kidney weights encountered upon lead administration improved after extract with A. aspera.
- Treatment with methanolic extract of A. aspera after lead induction completely ameliorated the lead-induced renal damage.
- Lead damage to the urine was evident from increase in the activity of enzymes such as gamma-glutamyltranspeptidase.
- Achyranthes aspera is described as an important herb in the Indian system of medicine.

CONTENTS
2
CODE
PROMOTING AN ETHICAL INDUSTRY
ABHI is the industry
association for the health
technology sector in the UK.
INTRODUCTION
3
The Association of British HealthTech Industries (ABHI) supports the health technology
community to provide products and services that help people live healthier lives.
With over 275 members, ABHI’s work is focused on showing the value of healthtech
and encouraging a healthy environment for economic growth. ABHI help Members
understand healthtech regulation and our work is underpinned by our Code of Ethical
Business Practice, which all Members adhere to.
The Code sets out the minimum standards appropriate to the various types of activities
carried out by the Members. The Code is not intended to supplant or supersede national
laws or regulations or professional codes (including company codes) that may impose
more stringent requirements upon Members and all Members should independently
ascertain that their activities comply with all current national and local laws, regulations
and professional codes.
Furthermore, Member Companies must be mindful of the fact that they may be
liable for the activities of third party intermediaries who interact with Healthcare
Professionals or Healthcare Organisations in connection with the sale, promotion or
other activity involving Member Companies’ products. Accordingly, it is recommended
that where such arrangements are entered into, the relevant contractual documentation
impose obligations upon the third party (for example, third party sales & marketing
intermediaries (SMIs), consultants, distributors, sales agents, marketing agents,
brokers, commissionaire commercial agents and independent sales representatives)
to comply with provisions set out in the Code or equivalent guidelines
1
.
KEY LEGISLATION
The health technology industry in the UK and Europe, in common with other
industries, is subject to national and supranational laws which govern many aspects
of their business operations. ABHI underlines compliance with the following laws and
regulations as having particular relevance to the health technology industry:
• Safety, Quality and Performance Laws;
•
Advertising and Promotion Laws;
•
Data Protection Laws;
•
Anti-corruption Laws;
•
Environmental Health and Safety Laws;
•
Competition Laws.
National and European Union (EU) competition legislation applies not only to Members
in their business operations, but also to ABHI, each of the association’s working groups
and any sub-group within the association, irrespective of size and name. Liability under
competition laws may be strict and a Member may become liable for the infringement
of such laws by other Members of an association group in which it participates.
Accordingly, Members must make every effort to observe UK and EU competition laws
in all their interactions.
1
. For further details, please refer to the Eucomed/AdvaMed Third Party SMIs guidance
4
CODEQUESTIONS AND ANSWERS
AIMS AND PRINCIPLES
OF THE CODE
The interaction between Members and Healthcare
Professionals and Healthcare Organisations is an important
feature in achieving ABHI’s mission to make safe, innovative
and reliable technology and related services available to
more people. For example:
• Advancement of
Health Technologies
The development of innovative medical devices,
technologies and in vitro diagnostics and the
improvement of existing products require collaboration
between Member Companies and Healthcare
Professionals and Healthcare Organisations. Innovation
and creativity are essential to the development and
evolution of health technologies and/or related services.
•
Safe and Effective Use
of Health Technology
The safe and effective use of health technology and
related services requires Member Companies to offer
Healthcare Professionals and Healthcare Organisations
appropriate instruction, education, training, service and
technical support.
•
Research and Education
Member Companies’ support of bona fide medical
research and education, serves to enhance Healthcare
Professionals’ clinical skills and thereby contribute to
patient safety and increase access to new technologies
and/or related services.
In each such interaction Member Companies must continue
to respect the obligation of Healthcare Professionals to
make independent decisions regarding treatment and
safeguard the environment in which the interaction takes
place to ensure the integrity of the industry. To achieve
this aim, the Code provides guidance on the interactions
of Member Companies with both Healthcare Professionals
and Healthcare Organisations, based upon the following
underlying principles:
Q1: Does the definition of Healthcare Professional
include purchasing professionals employed in the retail
sector, such as a purchasing professional employed by a
supermarket chain?
A1: No, the definition of Healthcare Professional does not include
a purchasing professional employed in the retail sector unless
that individual purchaser arranges for the purchase of Member
Companies’ medical devices for or on behalf of medical or clinical
personnel. For example, if a Member Company’s medical devices
are sold as part of the common merchandise of the retail outlet,
interactions between the Member Company and the purchasing pro-
fessional do not fall under the Code. However, where the Member
Company’s medical devices are sold in a retail pharmacy (even if
this is located within a supermarket unit), interactions between the
Member Company and the responsible purchasing professional will
fall under the Code.
5
CODEQUESTIONS AND ANSWERS
•
The Principle of Image
and Perception
Member Companies should, at all times, consider the
image and perception of the health technology industry
that will be projected to the public when interacting with
Healthcare Professionals and Healthcare Organisations.
•
The Principle of Separation
Interaction between industry and Healthcare
Professionals / Healthcare Organisations must not
be misused to influence through undue or improper
advantages, purchasing decisions, nor should such
interaction be contingent upon sales transactions or use
or recommendation of Member Companies’ products.
•
The Principle of Transparency
Interaction between industry and Healthcare
Professionals / Healthcare Organisations must be
transparent and comply with national and local laws,
regulations or professional codes of conduct. In
countries where specific provision is not made, Member
Companies shall nevertheless maintain appropriate
transparency by requiring prior written notification to the
hospital administration, the Healthcare Professional’s
superior or other locally-designated competent authority,
fully disclosing the purpose and scope of the interaction.
•
The Principle of Equivalence
Where Healthcare Professionals are engaged by a
Member Company to perform a service for or on behalf
of a Member Company, the remuneration paid by the
Member Company must be commensurate with, and
represent a fair market value for, the services performed
by the Healthcare Professional.
Q2: Must a Member Company require Employer
Notification to be given whenever Company personnel
meet HCPs at an HCO? (added in May 2017)
A2: No. Unless the Member Company’s interaction with an HCP
entails a transfer of value or raises a potential conflict of interest
there is no requirement for Employer Notification. However, Member
Companies must comply with any access requirements imposed by
HCOs to visiting Member Company personnel.
6
CODEQUESTIONS AND ANSWERS
•
The Principle of Documentation
For interactions between a Member Company and
a Healthcare Professional, such as where services
are performed by a Healthcare Professional for or on
behalf of a Member Company, there must be a written
agreement setting out, inter alia, the purpose of the
interaction, the services to be performed, the method for
reimbursement of expenses as well as the remuneration
to be paid by the Member Company. The activities
envisaged by the agreement must be substantiated
and evidenced by activity reports and the like. Adequate
documentation such as the agreement, related reports,
invoices etc. must be retained by the Member Company
for a reasonable period of time to support the need
for, and materiality of, the services as well as the
reasonableness of the remuneration paid.
INTERPRETING THE CODE
The use of capital letters indicates that a word or expression
is a defined term, the meaning of which is set out in the
Glossary.
Any phrase introduced by the terms: including, include, in
particular, or any similar expression shall be interpreted
as illustrative and shall not limit the sense of the words
preceding those terms.
ADMINISTERING THE CODE
The Code operates within a procedural framework which
includes procedures designed to provide an effective and
efficient complaint-handling process, to ensure compliance
with the Code. ABHI’s complaints handling system is based
on the principle that disputes are best resolved through
mediation.
The Conference Vetting System
1
is an independently-
managed system which reviews the compliance of Third
Party Organised Educational Events with the Code.
1.
For scope of application of CVS please refer to:
www.ethicalmedtech.eu
Q3: What is the Conference Vetting System (CVS) and,
is CVS approval required for all Third Party Organised
Educational Events before a Member Company can
provide support to these events? (added in May 2017)
A3: The Conference Vetting System (see the Glossary) has been
established as the online, binding and centralised decision-making
process to help Member Companies review the compliance of
relevant Third Party Organised Educational Events with the Code.
It is managed independently of the MedTech Europe Secretariat
and Members and is under the supervision of the MedTech
Europe Compliance Panel. CVS approval is only required for Third
Party Organised Educational Events which fall within its scope, as
provided here. Where there is a CVS decision in relation to a specific
Third Party Organised Educational Event, this decision is binding
upon all Member Companies.
7
CODEQUESTIONS AND ANSWERS
IMPLEMENTATION AND
TRANSITION PERIOD
This edition of the Code comes into force as follows:
• PART 4: The Complaints Procedure & Panel
Constitution shall enter into force on 1 January 2017;
and
• The balance of the Code i.e. Introduction, PART 1 and
PART 2, shall enter into force on 1 January 2018.
For the avoidance of doubt, during the transposition period
1 January 2017 to 31 December 2017, no material or activity
will be regarded as being in breach of the Code if it fails to
comply with its provisions only because of requirements
which this edition of the Code newly introduces.
TRANSITION PERIOD TO PHASE
OUT DIRECT SUPPORT FOR HCP
ATTENDANCE AT THIRD PARTY
ORGANISED EDUCATIONAL EVENTS
AND FOR HCP SPEAKERS
AT SATELLITE SYMPOSIA
After the end of the Transition Period (see the Glossary)
on 31 December 2018, Member Companies shall no
longer provide financial or in-kind support directly to
individual Healthcare Professionals to cover costs of their
attendance at Third Party Organised Educational Events
with the exception of Third Party Organised Procedure
Training meetings or pursuant to a consulting agreement
with a Healthcare Professional speaker engaged by a
Member Company to speak at a satellite symposium. This
means that support of individual Healthcare Professionals
to attend Third Party Organised Educational Events (as
provided for at Chapter 2, Section 3) shall no longer be
permitted under the Code.
After the Transition Period, Member Companies may
provide financial or in-kind support to Third Party Organised
Educational Events only through Educational Grants or
other types of funding in accordance with the rules of
Chapter 2: Third Party Organised Educational Events and
Chapter 4: Grants and Charitable Donations.
Q4: What is the difference between the Transposition
period and the Transition Period as defined in the
Glossary? (added in May 2017)
A4: Transposition means the process of incorporating the Code
within the Member Company’s own policy and procedures. This
process must be completed by 1 January 2018.
Transition Period means the period between 1 January 2017 and
31 December 2018 by the end of which Member Companies must
have ceased all financial or in-kind direct support to Healthcare
Professionals to attend Third Party Organised Educational
Conferences. Any exceptions to this rule are outlined in the Code
GUIDELINES ON THE INTERACTIONS
WITH HEALTHCARE PROFESSIONALS
AND HEALTHCARE ORGANISATIONS
PART 1
9
CODEQUESTIONS AND ANSWERS
GENERAL
CRITERIA
FOR EVENTS
1
Member Companies may invite Healthcare Professionals
to Company Events and Third Party Organised
Educational Events. The principles and criteria set out in
this Chapter 1 shall apply to all such Events supported
in any way by Member Companies, irrespective of who
organises the Event.
1. EVENT PROGRAMME
The Event programme should directly relate to the specialty
and/or medical practice of the Healthcare Professionals
who will attend the Event or be sufficiently relevant to justify
the attendance of the Healthcare Professionals. For Third
Party Organised Educational Events, the agenda should be
under the sole control and responsibility of the third party
organiser.
A Member Company shall not organise Events which
include social, sporting and/or leisure activities or other
forms of Entertainment, nor support such elements where
Q5: What is meant by “legitimate” or “genuine” as used
in the definitions of ‘Company Event’ and ‘Third Party
Organised Educational Conferences’?
A5: Any Event should be relevant to the Healthcare Professional
attendees; the detailed programme should be available sufficient
time prior to the Event; present a clear schedule with no gaps
during the sessions, (e.g., the minimum duration for a full day
Event should be 6 hours or 3 hours for a half day Event including
refreshment breaks). If it is a Third Party Organised Educational
Event the Faculty must be identified. It is also important that
all supporting materials (e.g. flyers, brochures and website)
are consistent with the scientific or promotional nature of the
programme content, as the case may be.
CODEQUESTIONS AND ANSWERS
10
CODEQUESTIONS AND ANSWERS
part of Third Party Organised Educational Events. For
Third Party Organised Educational Events, Entertainment
must be outside of the educational programme schedule
and paid for separately by the Healthcare Professionals.
Entertainment should not dominate or interfere with the
overall scientific content of the programme and must
be held during times that do not overlap with a scientific
session. The Entertainment should not be the main
attraction of the Third Party Organised Educational Event.
2. EVENT LOCATION AND VENUE
The Event location and venue should not become the main
attraction of the Event. For the location and the venue,
Member Companies must take into account at all times the
following considerations:
a. Potential adverse public perceptions of the location and
venue for the Event. The perceived image of the location
and venue must not be luxury, or tourist/holiday-oriented,
or that of an Entertainment venue.
b.
The Event location and venue should be centrally located
when regard is given to the place of residence of the
majority of invited participants.
c.
The need for ease of access for attendees.
d.
The Event location and venue should be in or near a city or
town which is a recognised scientific or business centre,
suitable for hosting an Event which is conducive to the
exchange of ideas and the transmission of knowledge.
e.
Member Companies must take into account the season
during which the Event is held. The selected time of year
must not be associated with a touristic season for the
selected geographic location.
Q6: Can a Member Company organise or support an
Event at a hotel or resort that offers significant leisure
facilities such as golf, casino or ski/water sports?
(Amended in July 2018)
A6: In principle, no. It is not appropriate for a Member Company to
organise or support Events at hotels or resorts renowned for their
entertainment facilities or centred around recreational or sporting
activities such as golf, private beach or ski/water sports.
Exceptions might be considered for venues well adapted to
business meetings in an otherwise compliant geographic location
where there is a compelling need to use the chosen venue, for
example, a lack of alternative venues or genuine safety or security
issues. In certain circumstances, hotel accommodation separate
from the Third-Party Organised Event venue might be required fo… [187236 chars truncated]
